steps.title: Use a common denominator
steps.content: The equation is a sum of reciprocals. Combine the terms on the right using the common denominator $17pqs$, then take the reciprocal of both sides to solve for $R$.

steps.title: Combine the right-side fractions over a common denominator
steps.content:
Starting from $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} + \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{17}$, use the common denominator $17pqs$:

$\frac{1}{p} = \frac{17qs}{17pqs}$, $\frac{1}{q} = \frac{17ps}{17pqs}$, $\frac{1}{s} = \frac{17pq}{17pqs}$, $\frac{1}{17} = \frac{pqs}{17pqs}$.

So $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{17qs + 17ps + 17pq + pqs}{17pqs}$.

steps.title: Take reciprocals to solve for R
Eliminate the answer choices
steps.content:
• A uses $p+q+s+17$ in the denominator, which would only be right if $1/R = 4/(p+q+s+17)$ — not equivalent to the given sum of reciprocals.
  • B equals $\frac{17pqs}{17qs+17ps+17pq+pqs}$, exactly what we derived.
  • C is the reciprocal of B, so it equals $\frac{1}{R}$, not $R$.
  • D sums the resistances directly, which is the formula for resistors in series, not parallel.
That confirms B is correct.
